name: Lozi
description: The Lozi are the people of Barotseland in western Zambia, whose Litunga (king) rules one of Africa's most impressive flood-plain kingdoms. The Barotse Flood Plain is a vast seasonal floodplain that covers 15,000 square kilometres; each year the Lozi move their capital between the high ground (winter capital Limulunga) and the flood plain (summer capital Lealui). This annual migration — the Kuomboka ceremony — is one of Africa's most celebrated living cultural events, featuring the royal barge Nalikwanda, decorated as a black and white elephant.
cultural_highlights: Kuomboka ceremony — the annual royal migration from flooded Lealui to high-ground Limulunga; the Litunga sails the royal barge Nalikwanda across a flooded plain; one of Africa's most spectacular ceremonies
Nalikwanda royal barge — decorated with a painted elephant, this ceremonial boat is the symbol of the Litunga's power
Kuta (royal court) system — a formal court with hereditary officials (indunas) who govern different aspects of Barotse life
Barotse flood plain ecology — a unique adaptive culture built entirely around seasonal flooding; agriculture, architecture, and calendar are all shaped by the annual flood

food_traditions.name: Nshima with fish
food_traditions.description: Thick maize porridge with fresh or smoked fish from the Zambezi and flood plain — the Lozi daily staple

food_traditions.name: Mukoyo (maize drink)
food_traditions.description: A sweet fermented drink made from dried roasted maize; served at ceremonies and as a cooling everyday drink

food_traditions.name: Flood plain vegetables
food_traditions.description: A variety of indigenous vegetables grown on the seasonally flooded plains; plant diversity is central to Lozi ecological knowledge

music_traditions: Silozi ngoma — ceremonial drumming accompanying the Kuomboka and other royal events
Makishi masquerade — masked ancestral figures performing during male initiation (mukanda) ceremonies across Barotseland
Sioma (royal drums) — enormous royal drums kept at the Kuta, struck only on specific ceremonial occasions
